title: Selective Annotation Makes Language Models Better Few-Shot Learners

structured.summary: The paper introduces a cost-effective two-step framework involving selective annotation and prompt retrieval to enhance few-shot learning performance in language models using the vote-k method. It demonstrates significant improvements in task performance with reduced annotation costs across multiple datasets and scenarios, including contexts of domain shift.

strengths.evidence: The framework improves performance while requiring 10-100 times less annotation (Abstract, Section 1).
Vote-k achieves a 12.9% relative gain with 18 annotated examples and 11.4% with 100 examples over random selection (Abstract, Section 1).

strengths.point: Robustness across model sizes and scenarios
strengths.evidence: The method consistently performs well across models from 2B to 175B and under domain shifts (Section 4).

weaknesses.point: Lack of novelty in the methodology
weaknesses.evidence: The method relies on established techniques like graph-based methods for diversity using Sentence-BERT embeddings (Section 2.1).
Key algorithmic details are deferred to Appendix G rather than being present in the main text (Section 2.1).
There is limited exploration of how vote-k compares with alternative active learning and selective annotation methods (Section 2.1).
Relative performance gains are reported without providing absolute scores or context (Abstract, Section 1).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient evaluation of diverse model architectures and sizes
weaknesses.evidence: Results for varying model sizes are mentioned but lack detailed discussion of individual implications (Section 4).

weaknesses.point: Dubious claims of robustness to domain shift
weaknesses.evidence: Minimal experimental details are provided on domain shift tests (Section 4.3).

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility concerns due to anonymized code
weaknesses.evidence: The code is available at an anonymized link, preventing immediate verification of results (Abstract).
There is no section dedicated to practical deployment challenges and computational costs (Results, General Discussion).

weaknesses.point: Assumed prevalence of large pools of unlabeled data
weaknesses.evidence: The methodology assumes easy access to unlabeled datasets without discussing potential constraints (Section 2).
Claims of cost savings lack detailed comparisons of time and resource expenditures (Section 4.1).
Potential biases and limitations due to the reliance on graph-based selection are not addressed (Section 2.1).
The scalability of the vote-k method for extremely large datasets is not discussed (Section 2.1).

weaknesses.point: Bias in datasets used for testing
weaknesses.evidence: The generalizability and fairness of the method may be affected by biases in dataset choices, which is not discussed (Section 3).

questions.question: What are the specific absolute performance metrics for tasks tested?
questions.motivation: Without absolute metrics, it is difficult to gauge the true effectiveness and relevance of the reported relative gains.

questions.question: How does vote-k compare with more recent or sophisticated alternative methods?
questions.motivation: Comprehensive benchmarking against current methods is necessary to establish the significance of vote-k's performance.

questions.question: What are the computational costs associated with implementing vote-k?
questions.motivation: Understanding real-world computational and resource costs is crucial for assessing the practical applicability of the method.

questions.question: How can the method be adapted for domains with limited access to large unlabeled datasets?
questions.motivation: Adapting the method for scenarios with data scarcity is essential for its broader applicability across different domains.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty in the methodology. The method relies on established techniques like graph-based methods for diversity using Sentence-BERT embeddings (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_002: Limited technical details in algorithm. Key algorithmic details are deferred to Appendix G rather than being present in the main text (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_003: Inadequate comparison with alternative methods. There is limited exploration of how vote-k compares with alternative active learning and selective annotation methods (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_004: Overemphasis on anecdotal performance gains. Relative performance gains are reported without providing absolute scores or context (Abstract, Section 1)
critique_points.point_005: Insufficient evaluation of diverse model architectures and sizes. Results for varying model sizes are mentioned but lack detailed discussion of individual implications (Section 4)
critique_points.point_006: Dubious claims of robustness to domain shift. Minimal experimental details are provided on domain shift tests (Section 4.3)
critique_points.point_007: Reproducibility concerns due to anonymized code. The code is available at an anonymized link, preventing immediate verification of results (Abstract)
critique_points.point_008: Insufficient discussion on real-world applicability. There is no section dedicated to practical deployment challenges and computational costs (Results, General Discussion)
critique_points.point_009: Assumed prevalence of large pools of unlabeled data. The methodology assumes easy access to unlabeled datasets without discussing potential constraints (Section 2)
critique_points.point_010: Oversimplified handling of annotation cost-effectiveness. Claims of cost savings lack detailed comparisons of time and resource expenditures (Section 4.1)
critique_points.point_011: Dependence on unsupervised graph techniques. Potential biases and limitations due to the reliance on graph-based selection are not addressed (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_012: Scalability concerns. The scalability of the vote-k method for extremely large datasets is not discussed (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_013: Bias in datasets used for testing. The generalizability and fairness of the method may be affected by biases in dataset choices, which is not discussed (Section 3)
